The longest run of 21.36km on April 12th, featuring a high average heart rate (172 bpm) and significant ascent (384m), was the most impactful session, contributing a 5.0 ATE. However, the overall distribution of intensity appears skewed towards moderate efforts, with few sessions pushing into higher ATE zones, which might limit progress in vVO2 and short, intense efforts. Recovery needs to be carefully monitored given the substantial volume and occasional high-intensity spikes, especially without specific recovery data.

mistakes: Lack of high-intensity anaerobic stimulus (average ATE of 0.8) suggests potential underdevelopment of vVO2 and short, flat distance capabilities.

recommendations: Incorporate 1-2 dedicated high-intensity interval sessions per week (e.g., hill sprints, track intervals, or short, sharp efforts on trails) to improve vVO2 and anaerobic capacity.

Consider tracking recovery metrics (HRV, sleep quality, subjective fatigue) to ensure training load is sustainable and to identify potential overtraining.

Periodize intensity more deliberately; ensure a build-up and recovery cycle within blocks of training, rather than sporadic high-effort days.
